text: Well, it's been quite the journey, dear viewer. 10 years, five seasons, and over 50 hours of TV all brought us to this point. And all of it came down to one question. Would Stranger Things managed to stick the land in? And now that I've seen all 128 minutes of this show's finale, the answer is complicated. On the one hand, it's completely overblown, overwritten, melodramatic, and self-indulgent to the point of parody. The final confrontation is basically a Marvel movie with portals

and sky beams and superheroes and giant kaijus rampaging across alien planets. Half the insanely bloated cast could best be described as well. We had to give them something to do. It's probably going to be forever overshadowed in people's minds by Will's stupid coming out scene that was so bursting with emotionally manipulative earnestness that it felt more like a Hallmark commercial than a sci-fi mystery show. And considering the epilogue is almost an hour long by itself, a whole lot of

things get conveniently forgotten about so the writers could have a nice neat ending. But I did kind of like it. I know, I know, maybe I'm just getting soft in my old age. Maybe I'm falling for the emotional manipulation of a show that's become nostalgic for itself. But the fact is, it's kind of earned it at this point. Stranger Things has been around for almost a quarter of my life. And although it hasn't exactly been a constant presence, the ending does feel

like a significant event that's treated with all the gravitas it deserves and then some. Now, I'm going to talk about the plot here, so if you don't want any spoilers for this show, I suggest you run off and hide under the bed, you [ __ ] [ __ ] Anyway, let's begin. The finale kicks off with Vecna using Holly and the other kidnapped children as psychic battery packs to merge our dimension with an alien one where the mind flare lives. Because it turns out the upside down is actually just a meat

tunnel that connects the two worlds. And the only way to stop him is for 11 Max and whatever that other girl's called to interrupt the ritual by traveling through Vecna's memories inside his heads. And reading that sentence back now, holy [ __ ] that has to be absolutely baffling to anyone who hasn't seen the show. It did make me wonder though how his memories even function in this world because wouldn't he only remember stuff that he directly experienced at the time? Like events

taking place in other rooms or other locations would just be blank because he wasn't there to see and remember them. >> Don't ask questions. >> Ah, whatever. So, they get to the room where the ritual's happening and they save the kids just as the dimensions start to merge, allowing Rambo Nancy to lead the rest of the groovy gang up into the abyss because I guess you can just physically climb into whole other dimensions now where they start searching for Vecna's lair so that they

can kill his physical body. Why is this 90 lb girl who can barely even hold a gun suddenly the most badass soldier who ever lived? I don't know. They had to give her something to do, I guess. Why are the rest of the gang armed with spears and Molotov cocktails instead of oh, I don't know, even more guns? Don't know. But then Hopper gets fooled into blowing a hole in the isolation tank where Eleven is operating out of, which takes her out of Vecna's mind. But

that's okay because then she just kind of leaprogs up into the abyss anyway, just in time for a big confrontation with the bad guy. And I can't quite believe that this sequence is even real. But she flies through the air like Captain Marvel and blasts her way inside its body. >> [snorts] >> Really? [laughter] >> If you ever wanted an example of power creep in longunning narratives, this is what it looks like, boys and girls. So Nancy and the Groovy Gang hatch a

cunning plan to damage it by shooting it a lot. You sure about that? You sure about that? Do you really think an M16 is going to have any impact on something the size of the Empire State Building? My favorite part of this five minutes of pure insanity has got to be Steve and Dustin who for some reason get right underneath it and start stabbing it with spears. Like you understand that neither of you are possibly getting out of this in one piece, right? Or if you do, it's going

to be one big flat piece. Considering how powerful 11 has become now, it also kind of makes you wonder why she didn't just rip its [ __ ] head off or crush its heart into dust or something. But whatever. Stranger Things has always had trouble with big climactic confrontations like this because they were pretty much always resolved with Eleven pointing their hand at the camera in slow motion and screaming really loud. But again, they had to give everyone else something to do. So, this

is what we end up with. I guess the upshot of all this madness is that she's struggling to fight Vecna all by herself. But then Will stops crying long enough to show up and harness the power of the big gay to push him into a [ __ ] spike and take him out. Then Joyce shows up for some reason to finish him by chopping his head off because they had to give her something to do as well. >> Fatality. >> Then they all escape back into the real world, but oh no, Linda Hamilton and the

military guys are there to arrest them. So Eleven magically teleports over to the portal. Hm. That all seems a bit suspicious, doesn't it? Just as a big bomb goes off to destroy the upside down and seal the gateway forever. Then I guess the military just kind of forgets about the whole thing because 18 months later everyone's happy and free and graduating from high school. I mean, you understand these people are responsible for subverting highly classified military operations, sabotaging vital

missions, destroying equipment, and killing literally dozens of active duty personnel. Right? In the best case scenario, they're all going to be in prison for the rest of their natural lives. >> Don't ask questions. Let's not worry about that, though, because the Duffer brothers certainly didn't. They had more important stuff to deal with. Remember that love triangle between Nancy and Steve and Jonathan that's been going for like 10 years now? Well, we find out

that Nancy ditches both men to marry something way more important, a meaningless corporate job where she'll gradually sink into a lonely, unsatisfied middle age of late night TV and wine boxes before dying alone in her fashionable but soulless apartment, childless and unloved. her only legacy, a couple of domestic cats that will slowly feast on her decomposing corpse. >> He's out of line, but he's right. >> Oh, yeah. And it turns out that Eleven faked her own death to escape the

military and start a new life. I guess on the assumption that >> they will find you unless they think you're already dead. So, she moves to some small village in Norway or something, which kind of had me scratching my head because small isolated communities like that are tightly knit and the arrival of an outsider would be talked about and probably spread as a rumor to other places. It would have made much more sense for her to settle in a big city where she's surrounded by millions of

anonymous people, but ah well, who cares? And the show ends with one final game of Dn D where Mike and the others get super emotional because they realize this is probably the last time they'll ever be relevant as actors before turning the board over to the next generation. And that's it. That's Stranger Things. If I had to sum up this finale and to some extent the whole season, I'd say it reminds me of some old guy that's worked at the same company for like 50 years given his

retirement speech that goes on a bit too long and is a bit too sappy and self-indulgent. But what the hell? you're willing to hear the guy out because he's kind of earned it. With Game of Thrones final season, you could absolutely tell the showrunners were trying to speedrun their way to the end because they were bored of the show by that point and desperate to move on to bigger and better things. How did that work out for you chaps? I definitely can't accuse the Duffer brothers of

pulling that kind of [ __ ] here, though. If anything, they seem to have the opposite problem. trying to draw things out a little too long, getting a little too sentimental and schmaltzy, playing it all a little too safe so they can tie everything up with a neat little narrative bow. I don't hate it, and I don't blame them for wanting to end on a positive note. But it's all a bit predictable and conventional in a show that was always about doing the unexpected and playing with your

assumptions. As I mentioned before in my season 5 review, the biggest issue with this show is the lack of character discipline. The Duffer brothers collect characters like other people collect Pokémon cards. And by the finale, it was getting to absurd levels. Even with all of the convoluted [ __ ] going on, and believe me, there was lots of that. There was just way too many people and not enough for them to do. The result is that some characters vanished for half

the episode. Some got put into a holding pattern and others just kind of stood around waiting for something to happen. I really expected them to kill off a few people to streamline the narrative or just give it a bit of weight and meaning, but nope. Apart from Eleven's friend, who was so lacking in personality that I couldn't even be bothered to look up her name, every single character makes it to the end credits. There's some attempt to introduce more depth to Henry as an

antagonist by fleshing out his origin story. But all it really does is raise more questions the show doesn't have the time or the scope to answer now. So, he gets exposed to a fragment of the mind flare that corrupts his mind. A how? Where did it come from? How did that scientist end up down a [ __ ] minehaft in the middle of the desert? Who was he running away from? And why? Why did the demigorgans stop existing when they were no longer convenient to the plot? Why

did the upside down go from a toxic monster-filled hellscape to a place you can just hang out with no issues? What was the military's ultimate goal with this whole operation? Why wouldn't they work with the other characters to take on a massive existential threat like Vecna and the mind flare? What happened to Linda Hamilton after this was all over? Why was Max brought back to life when she serves almost no purpose in the story? Why does Millie Bobby Brown have

such a weird facial expression? The problem with any mystery show is that the answers to the mystery are usually less interesting than the questions it posed. And I think Stranger Things is in the weird position of giving slightly unsatisfying answers while also generating a whole bunch of brand new questions that it probably didn't intend to ask. It's not an ideal solution. But when all said and done, well, it could have been worse. A lot worse. It definitely wasn't the triumphant finale

that redeemed a pretty flawed and frustrating season, but it also didn't [ __ ] the bed in spectacular fashion. I don't know, man. Maybe it had got too big, too expensive, and the stakes were just too high to do anything truly bold or controversial. So, what we got instead was a slightly overblown ending that was heavy on spectacle and sentimentality that's unlikely to blow many people away, but probably isn't going to piss too many people off either. Did it stick the landing? Well,

it definitely wobbled and maybe took a step back to steady itself, but it did at least go out on its feet. And in this day and age, that's an achievement in itself. And now that it's all over, so long, Stranger Things, it's been well, it's been real. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
